review_text:
My first 1 star review goes to...the very deserving Circus Circus.  This review has everything to do with their employees and nothing to do with the amenities of the hotel and casino.

We all know how fun it is to relive your childhood upstairs where all the games and circus shows go down so my friends and I spent some time up there.  We sat down to play Circus Downs, the game where you roll a ball into holes to try to get your horse across the board, but there was a cheater amongst us.  He stole a ball..and used 2 to play.  The worker did nothing to stop him and even awarded that jerk a prize at the end.  Seriously?! Little kids are playing this game and you're going to cheat them from a fair game??  When I asked the employee to let us play another round, he just ignored me!  He didn't even apologize for the cheater or simply say no.  A friend and I decided to report this situation to security...which turned out to be no help AT ALL.  

Us: *tap* Excuse me.
Security biatch: Don't touch me.
Us: I need to report an incident. We were upstairs-
Security: *with LOTS of attitude* Upstairs? Upstairs where? Midway? 
Us: Um...upstairs where all the gam..
Security: Get to the point. What.Do.You.Want?
*Oh hellll no! At this point..I've had it up to HERE with her attitude!*
Us: If you're busy and you need to be somewhere, then I can talk to someone else.  Just point me to the direction of a manager.
Security: *throws back her jacket flap to reveal her "badge* Security.
Us: Tells her the story...blah blah 
Security: What? So this is about a teddy bear? 
Us: NO, this about ethics and the way the employee inappropriately handled the situation.
Security: How old are you?
Us:......Why does that matter?
Security: I have the right to ask.

WHAT A BEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEE!!! Really?! You're a security guard and you're going around riding on your high horse??  Are you even doing your job securing the premises? I wish I had gotten HER name so that I could report her!!

As you can guess, talking to the manager of midway was pointless.  He apologized for the "terrible predicament" we were in and how he understands "it sucks."  WHY ARE THEY TALKING TO US AS IF WE'RE 8 YEARS OLD?! 

adijgoiasjdgoiasdg CIRCUS CIRCUS YOU GUYS SUCK!!!
issue: Employee ignored cheating, allowed the cheater to win a prize, and failed to address the complaint or apologize
fixes: Deploy a mandatory “Cheating Incident Reporting” form in the company’s ServiceNow incident module, requiring the employee who observed cheating to log details (time, location, witness) within 24 hours and automatically routing the case to the Ethics Review Team for immediate investigation. | - Institute a “Zero‑Tolerance Cheating Policy” with clear escalation steps outlined in the HR Policy Handbook, and integrate the policy into the existing LMS (e.g., Cornerstone) so all staff complete a 10‑minute refresher quiz annually, with completion tracked via the LMS reporting dashboard. | - Create a dedicated “Cheating Resolution” workflow in Zendesk that triggers a 48‑hour SLA for the case owner to acknowledge receipt, schedule a call with the complainant, and close the ticket with a signed apology and documented corrective action before it is archived. | - Schedule a monthly “Cheating Accountability Review” meeting where the Compliance Officer reviews every logged cheating incident, assigns corrective actions (including potential disciplinary measures), and publishes a KPI report showing zero repeat violations for the past quarter on the internal SharePoint dashboard.
rationale: This response excels in providing actionable recommendations that target the root cause of the issue. It specifies how to implement each recommendation and refers to concrete tools such as ServiceNow, LMS (Cornerstone), Zendesk, and SharePoint. The suggestions are clearly outlined and would enable immediate execution by frontline workers without requiring further clarification or questions. Moreover, the response demonstrates a deep understanding of the task requirements, presenting a structured plan that adheres to the given theme and customer issue. Each recommendation is feasible, with no significant obstacles to implementation. Therefore, according to the rubric's criteria for relevance, actionability, concreteness, and feasibility, this response fulfills all the criteria perfectly.

review_text:
My review pertains only to the Pet Boarding facilities.

Their "Hound Hotel" sounds/looks really good online, but in person it was a real let down.  The kennel is in a room off the parking garage, steps from the city sidewalk.  I was told it was by a security desk, but when we arrived, that whole tower was closed and unattended.  If somone were able to get into the room, they could get to your dog through the space at the top of the cage, even though the cage was locked.  The cameras are a nice feature, but are you gonna watch the dog 24/7?  Even if you saw someone doing something with your dog, it would take you 10 minutes to get there from the other tower.  We packed up and went to the Reno Plaza hotel, who allows pets in the room.

If you care for your pet, you would not use these facilities.
issue: The online description of the "Hound Hotel" is misleading and does not match the actual facility experience
fixes: Deploy a cross‑functional review workflow in Confluence where product, copywriting, and site‑ops sign off on every listing before publication; require a “Match‑Check” checklist that references the current property inventory database (e.g., PropertyGuru) and blocks release if any discrepancy exceeds ±5 % of advertised amenities. | - Integrate Google Street View and indoor‑mapping tours into the “Hound Hotel” listing via the CMS (WordPress + Yoast SEO), then run a monthly quality‑audit script in Selenium to verify that all visuals remain up‑to‑date with the live facility layout. | - Launch a post‑stay verification email triggered by the CRM (HubSpot) that asks guests to rate accuracy on a 1‑5 scale; automatically flag listings scoring below 3 and route them to a dedicated “Listing Accuracy” task queue in Asana for immediate remediation within 48 hours. | - Implement a real‑time content monitoring dashboard in Power BI that pulls data from the hotel’s PMS (Opera) and the website’s content API; set automated alerts when advertised room types, amenity counts, or pricing deviate more than 10 % from the live offering, prompting immediate content edits through the CMS user interface.
rationale: The recommendations are actionable and specific, addressing the exact problem of the hotel’s misleading descriptions. Each recommendation includes concrete tools such as Confluence, Google Street View, Selenium, HubSpot, Asana, Power BI, and Opera, demonstrating a clear understanding of how to implement solutions in practice. Furthermore, these recommendations can be easily executed by the frontline workers without requiring additional interpretation or effort. The feasibility of the proposed solutions is generally low, with most of the tools and processes already in place. However, there are minor configurations required, such as setting up a real-time monitoring dashboard in Power BI, which could require some effort to integrate with existing systems. Despite these minor issues, the overall feasibility is quite acceptable.

review_text:
Ahhh Circus Circus Reno, lets take a tour shall we?  Keep in mind we're not expecting much, this IS Reno, not Vegas.

Lets check in!  Go to the front desk and be greeted by the most somber and unpleasant reception desk staff I've encountered.  Pretty much everybody we talk to up there seem to hate their life and made sure to show it.  What a great vibe to start our trip!

Take a walk through the casino floor on the way to the room.  Take a look around.  The wait staff looks like they've been working there since 1975, jaw-jacked clientele super focused on their slot game nearing the end of their 72 hour meth bender (bet your ass they're staying in the room next to you!).  Outstanding!  Unless you're waiting for the tram to take you to the Sky Tower (which "sometimes" works), looks like you're walking about 5-10 minutes to your room carrying all your shit, probably should have just got a bellhop, but expect the same bad customer service, so we'll do it ourselves.

Arriving in the room, you'll be greeted by questionable bedding and furniture that looks like it's been sitting in the room since about the time that the wait staff was hired (circa 1975 or so... at least the TV was from the late 80s or early 90s).  You might even be lucky enough to find some dirty towels in the bathroom.  If you forgot your lighter in the car you're shit outta luck, since there's no matches to be found in the room.

Spend about 60 minutes downstairs and get stared at by creepy crackheads and ignored by wait staff.  Lets go get a drink at the bar!  Why does the bartender act like I'm unconvincing him be even being there?  There's no "Hey bud what can I get cha" here, just a blank stare and a nod acknowledging that you did, in fact, place an order.

...


Word to the wise:  Go ahead and stay at any of the other Casinos apart from the Circus Circus-Silver Legacy-El Dorado group.  I'd recommend paying a little extra and staying at the Peppermill or Atlantis (Reno's best "Vegas-like" experience), or if you have to stay downtown, go ahead and stay at the Sands Regency, at least the staff is more friendly and the rooms are bigger, for a cheaper price.  Clientele is slightly on the shady side as well, but it's Reno, I wouldn't expect anything more.
issue: Reception and front‑desk staff were unfriendly and displayed a negative attitude
fixes: Deploy a weekly “Mystery Guest” program through the **Medallia Experience Management** platform, scheduling unannounced visits to capture real‑time interaction scores; tie any score < 7 to mandatory coaching within 48 hours. | - Launch a **15‑minute daily huddle** on the **Microsoft Teams Frontline** app where the shift lead reviews a brief video clip of positive guest interactions from the previous day and assigns one staff member to deliver a quick “attitude boost” micro‑training before their next shift. | - Integrate **Kronos Workforce Central** with a **real‑time sentiment dashboard** (e.g., via a Zapier workflow) that flags any “negative tone” tag from call recordings to trigger an immediate supervisor review and corrective action plan within 24 hours. | - Implement a **monthly gamified recognition program** in **Bonusly**, awarding points for “positive feedback cards” collected by QR codes at checkout; staff who accumulate ≥10 points per month receive a $50 gift card and are entered into a quarterly “Attitude Champion” draw.

Clientele is slightly on the shady side as well, but it's Reno, I wouldn't expect anything more.
issue: Room furnishings were outdated, bedding and towels were dirty, and basic amenities like matches were missing
fixes: Deploy a digital housekeeping checklist in iAuditor (or a similar mobile audit app) that requires staff to photograph and sign off on each item (bedding, towels, matches) before marking a room “ready,” with automatic alerts if any task is missed. | - Contract a quarterly furniture refresh program through a vetted vendor portal (e.g., ServiceNow Supplier Management) that schedules replacement of at least 20 % of high‑traffic pieces per quarter, tracked by purchase orders and inventory tags. | - Install QR‑code stationery in every room that links to a real‑time amenity replenishment dashboard in the property management system; housekeepers scan the code when they restock matches, which logs the action and triggers low‑stock notifications. | - Introduce a post‑checkout linen quality scorecard collected via the Guest Experience Survey (targeting ≥95 % cleanliness rating) that feeds into a weekly performance KPI report reviewed by the Housekeeping Manager.
rationale: The response provides specific and actionable recommendations using concrete tools such as iAuditor, ServiceNow Supplier Management, QR-code stationery, and the Guest Experience Survey. Each recommendation effectively addresses the issue of room cleanliness by suggesting practical steps like a digital housekeeping checklist, a quarterly furniture refresh program, and real-time amenity replenishment tracking. Furthermore, these recommendations are feasible with minimal cost or effort, aligning with the budget-conscious requirement of the task. Additionally, each recommendation is within the specified length limit of 1-2 sentences, maintaining clarity without unnecessary detail.

review_text:
Ahhh Circus Circus Reno, lets take a tour shall we?  Keep in mind we're not expecting much, this IS Reno, not Vegas.

Lets check in!  Go to the front desk and be greeted by the most somber and unpleasant reception desk staff I've encountered.  Pretty much everybody we talk to up there seem to hate their life and made sure to show it.  What a great vibe to start our trip!

Take a walk through the casino floor on the way to the room.  Take a look around.  The wait staff looks like they've been working there since 1975, jaw-jacked clientele super focused on their slot game nearing the end of their 72 hour meth bender (bet your ass they're staying in the room next to you!).  Outstanding!  Unless you're waiting for the tram to take you to the Sky Tower (which "sometimes" works), looks like you're walking about 5-10 minutes to your room carrying all your shit, probably should have just got a bellhop, but expect the same bad customer service, so we'll do it ourselves.

Arriving in the room, you'll be greeted by questionable bedding and furniture that looks like it's been sitting in the room since about the time that the wait staff was hired (circa 1975 or so... at least the TV was from the late 80s or early 90s).  You might even be lucky enough to find some dirty towels in the bathroom.  If you forgot your lighter in the car you're shit outta luck, since there's no matches to be found in the room.

Spend about 60 minutes downstairs and get stared at by creepy crackheads and ignored by wait staff.  Lets go get a drink at the bar!  Why does the bartender act like I'm unconvincing him be even being there?  There's no "Hey bud what can I get cha" here, just a blank stare and a nod acknowledging that you did, in fact, place an order.

...

issue: The tram to the Sky Tower was unreliable and often did not work
fixes: Deploy IoT vibration sensors (e.g., Siemens MindSphere) on each tram car and integrate the data feed into a real‑time monitoring dashboard; set automated alerts when sensor thresholds exceed acceptable levels so maintenance crews can dispatch within 15 minutes. | - Create a predictive‑maintenance workflow in ServiceNow CMMS that schedules inspections based on usage hours and temperature/humidity cycles, generating work orders with SLA = 48 hours for critical components such as motors and brakes. | - Install QR‑code‑linked iAuditor checklists at each stop station for staff to perform a 5‑minute pre‑shift diagnostic; aggregate results daily in Power BI to identify recurring failure patterns and trigger corrective actions. | - Contract a local supplier of spare parts and negotiate a “same‑day delivery” agreement, then establish a Kanban inventory rule in SAP MM that automatically replenishes stock when part usage exceeds a predefined threshold (e.g., 10 % of total parts consumed). | - Launch a passenger‑feedback loop via the existing Sky Tower mobile app (using Firebase Cloud Messaging) that logs missed‑service incidents and routes them to a dedicated Slack channel monitored by the operations team, ensuring resolution within 24 hours.

review_text: Raquel, Brittney, & some middle eastern dude that works in the VIP downstairs....all terrible customer service. As a VIP member I should be receiving better care. They were all rude and unhelpful. I will never stay here again. I can not believe this service. It takes over an hour after talking to 4 different people and I still don't have a wifi connection after I paid for it? Not okay.
issue: Rude and unhelpful staff providing poor customer service, especially for VIP members
fixes: Deploy a real‑time sentiment dashboard in Zendesk (or Freshdesk) that flags any ticket with a negative CSAT score < 3; automatically route those tickets to a “VIP Escalation” queue and trigger a 24‑hour follow‑up email from the account manager. | - Launch weekly 45‑minute VR empathy workshops on Strivr, where agents don’t just learn scripts but experience first‑hand what it feels like to be a VIP member, and record completion rates in the LMS for compliance tracking. | - Institute a “Voice‑of‑the‑Customer” program using Medallia surveys after every interaction, setting a minimum 90% positive rating threshold for VIP contacts and linking results to quarterly performance bonuses tied to individual scores. | - Create a dedicated VIP support team managed through a RACI matrix in Asana, assigning each agent a fixed number of VIP cases per shift and measuring adherence via a daily KPI report that flags any deviation >10%. | - Implement automated post‑interaction NPS nudges via Twilio SMS that ask VIP customers to rate the interaction instantly, feeding results into a Power BI dashboard updated in real time so managers can address issues within 48 hours.
rationale: The response provided actionable recommendations that are clearly defined and specific to the customer issue. Each recommendation includes a tool or system, such as Zendesk, Freshdesk, Strivr, LMS, Medallia surveys, Asana, Twilio SMS, and Power BI dashboard. The recommendations not only address the root cause of the problem but also propose measurable actions like CSAT scores, VR empathy workshops, voice-of-the-customer program, dedicated support team management through a RACI matrix, and automated NPS nudges. Furthermore, the response is well-structured with each recommendation being 1-2 sentences long. However, while most recommendations are feasible, some may require a moderate budget or cross-department coordination which slightly deviates from the highest score criteria for feasibility.

review_text:
I work in Reno Feb-March, for the last ....years, beds have not been upgraded for many years, the air quality is very bad, especially for one whose profession is a vocalist.  haven't tried the food here,  my room had a broken heater and had to be replaced.  All in all, Would never stay here, but it's provided by my employer, so that's the catch.  don't know how Reno will sustain these large hotel casinos with such poor entertainment.

I'm on the road about 6 months a year throughout North America and Europe, and about 95% of hotels have free wifi.  Circus Circus charges $10.00 per day for wifi, an added out of pocket expense for business travellers.  For one who must make many international calls, free wifi is a must for making travel reservations.  The only hotels charging for wifi these days are the big budget hotels, or downtown Reno.  Just Sayin', time to change Circus Circus.   Not happy paying for premium wireless, when I can't log on to internet banking or try to watch a news video of a dangerous high wind weather alert.  Just a note to all of you who like comfortable new beds,  I would go with El Dorado.  They have new flat screen TVs, but Circus Circus still has the 1990 model.  You'd think with all of that gambling revenue, they could upgrade some important things.  Hotel premium wireless is $13.00 per day,  download speeds are .31 MBPS,  You might be able to watch a 30 second video in 3 hours at that rate.  Unacceptable.  
On a positive note, some good live music in the Lounge.
issue: Beds are outdated and uncomfortable, not upgraded for years.
fixes: Deploy a quarterly “Bed Audit” checklist in Asana (or Trello) with standardized comfort criteria (e.g., mattress firmness, pillow loft, headboard condition) and assign the housekeeping manager to complete it before each guest check‑in. | - Replace all mattresses older than 5 years with CertiPur‑US certified memory foam models sourced through a vetted supplier contract (e.g., Sleep Number), tracking purchase orders in SAP MM to ensure 100 % compliance within 12 months. | - Install QR‑code feedback stations on each bed that feed real‑time comfort ratings into Medallia; set a KPI that ≥85 % of responses rate beds as “comfortable” and trigger automatic work orders in ServiceNow when scores fall below that threshold. | - Create a phased upgrade schedule in Microsoft Project that allocates a fixed $X per room per year for new bedding and sets a target of upgrading 20 % of rooms each quarter, with progress reviewed in the monthly operations dashboard.
rationale: The response is mostly well-structured, but it could have benefitted from a few more recommendations to reach the full number of 3-5. Additionally, while the recommendations are generally actionable and concrete, some minor interpretation might be required for certain steps, like specifying the process of integrating QR-code feedback stations into Medallia. The feasibility score could also have been improved by detailing the cost implications and potential challenges in implementing these suggestions.

review_text: My fiancé and I will no longer be valued customers as we were not treat with respect. Worst experience ever!!!

fixes: Deploy a real‑time sentiment monitoring dashboard in Zendesk (or Freshdesk) that flags any ticket flagged “disrespectful” by customers, automatically routing it to a senior supervisor within 15 minutes for immediate follow‑up. | - Launch mandatory quarterly microlearning modules on respect etiquette hosted on TalentLMS, requiring a ≥90 % pass rate on the built‑in quiz before agents can handle live chats again. | - Introduce a peer‑review program where every agent records one live call per week using CallMiner analytics; supervisors score the interaction against a Respect Scorecard and provide written feedback within two business days. | - Implement a “Respect KPI” in Power BI that tracks the percentage of tickets resolved without disrespect complaints, with a monthly target of ≤0.5 % and automatic alerts when the threshold is breached.

review_text:
I give it 2 stars only because it was so darn cheap.  I stayed there for 30 a night. I mean c'mon.  It really doesn't get cheaper than that - even regular motels are more expensive. 

The games there aren't what they used to be when I went as a child and they're so much more pricey.  It's fun to play them again though and win those useless prizes. I actually had fun playing the arcade games where you win tickets and trade them in though.  They still have the old school games that I don't even remember the names of because they're that old.   The shows were so half assed but I wasn't expecting an extravagant show anyway.  It's sad because reno is so empty unless there's an event going on.  The food in that vicinity wasn't that great either and I noticed while and after eating that everyone that was there was 60+.  It's a great meat market for the seniors! haha
issue: The motel is extremely cheap, suggesting low quality and poor value.
fixes: Deploy a quarterly competitive‑pricing audit in Price2Spy to benchmark nightly rates against local rivals; adjust the motel’s price tier by no more than 5 % weekly until the average rate aligns with market standards without compromising occupancy. | - Launch a “Value Bundle” offering (e.g., free Wi‑Fi + continental breakfast) managed through the property management system (PMS) and track uptake via the built‑in analytics dashboard to ensure ≥ 70 % bundle adoption within two months. | - Implement a post‑stay NPS survey integrated with Medallia, targeting a minimum score of 8/10 on perceived value; feed results into a weekly “value‑gap” report that triggers corrective pricing actions when the gap exceeds 15 %. | - Create a dynamic pricing rule set in Duetto that adjusts room rates based on real‑time demand signals (weather, events) while keeping the lowest price at least 10 % above the market median, measured by daily revenue variance reports.
rationale: The response addresses the specific issue of pricing and offers actionable recommendations that are relevant to the customer's exact problem. The recommendations use specific tools such as Price2Spy, Medallia, Duetto, and the built-in PMS analytics dashboard, which demonstrates a clear understanding of the task requirements. Each recommendation is not only concrete but also feasible for implementation with minimal effort required. The response maintains a high level of actionability and relevance throughout, making it easy for a frontline worker to execute immediately without asking questions. The response does not default to a score of 4 and instead justifies every 4 or 5 in the rubric.

I give it 2 stars only because it was so darn cheap.  I stayed there for 30 a night. I mean c'mon.  It really doesn't get cheaper than that - even regular motels are more expensive.

review_text:
This review is on the kids Midway game area:

As a child, I lived for Circus Circus! My family would come here several times a year and it was amazing and fun.

I took my 9 year old son here this past weekend for the first time and it was garbage to how it was before. They did not at all upgrade this place. They even took out one of the popular games Fantasia! The shows they had going on werent that entertaining. The games were getting overpriced and there wasn't really any new and fun exciting games. My son basically got bored in an hour and really wasn't that entertained by everything. I had to agree with him big time because I knew it was trash compared to what it was before. They need to do a huge upgrade to this place! I'm sure if they did, they would get a lot more visitors.
issue: The kids Midway area has not been upgraded and feels outdated compared to its past condition
fixes: Create a phased renovation roadmap in Asana with Gantt chart timelines and assign a cross‑functional team (marketing, operations, finance) to deliver new lighting, flooring, and interactive digital signage within Q1 2024. | - Launch a QR‑code‑linked micro‑survey on the existing Midway screens every two weeks to capture real‑time guest preferences, then feed the data into a Power BI dashboard for weekly decision‑making. | - Partner with a local design agency to develop a modular “pop‑up” play zone using pre‑built, stackable units (e.g., inflatable obstacle course) that can be installed and removed in under 48 hours for seasonal rotations. | - Introduce a gamified loyalty program through the existing mobile app that awards points for time spent at the Midway; set a KPI of 20 % increase in average dwell time measured via foot‑traffic sensors after six months.
rationale: The response is generally well-structured and aligns closely with the task requirements. Each recommendation specifically targets a concrete aspect of service quality, and each action step within the recommendations is well-defined and immediately executable by frontline workers. The real-world tools mentioned, such as Asana, Power BI, and foot-traffic sensors, are precise and tangible. Additionally, the feasibility score demonstrates an understanding of the project's potential resource constraints and timeframes.
